questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe negation-as-failure (NOT) in logic programming? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: NOT X succeeds if and only if X cannot be proved from the current KB.
NOT X is equivalent to classical negation $\neg X$.
A rule of the form h(X) :- b(X), NOT c(X) concludes h(X) when b(X) holds and c(X) cannot be proved.
NOT X succeeds if X is provably false.

explanations.text: Negation-as-failure succeeds when the system cannot derive the goal, not when it is classically false.
explanations.notes_quote: NOT, meaning 'cannot be proved.'

explanations.text: Logic programs do not use strict classical negation. NOT is weaker — it only reflects unprovability, not falsity.
This is exactly the reading of a logic programming rule with negation-as-failure.
explanations.notes_quote: h(X) :- b_1(X), b_2(X), NOT c(X) meaning: conclude h(X) if b_1(X) and b_2(X) hold and c(X) cannot be proved.

explanations.text: NOT X does not require X to be provably false — only that X cannot be proved. These differ under an open-world assumption.

questions.text: Which statements about the logic programming consequence relation KB |~ Q are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: KB |~ Q holds if the query ?Q returns true for some instantiation of variables in Q.
The relation is monotonic: if KB |~ Q then KB union {R} |~ Q for any R.
Adding new rules to KB can invalidate previously derivable consequences.
The relation is defined only for ground (variable-free) queries.

explanations.text: The consequence relation holds when Prolog returns true for some variable instantiation.
explanations.notes_quote: if and only if the query ?Q returns true for some instantiation of variables in Q.

explanations.text: The relation is explicitly non-monotonic — adding rules can block previously-firing NOT conditions.
This is the defining feature of non-monotonic consequence — the Tweety trilogy demonstrates this.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
explanations.notes_quote: This demonstrates non-monotonicity: adding a formula to the KB invalidates a previously derivable consequence.

explanations.text: The definition allows variable instantiation, so non-ground queries are permitted.

questions.source_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
questions.text: In KB1 (penguin(tweety), bird(X):-penguin(X), fly(X):-bird(X),NOT abnormal_fly(X)), which of the following hold? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: KB1 |~ fly(tweety) because abnormal_fly(tweety) cannot be proved.
KB1 |~ fly(tweety) because tweety is classically entailed to fly.
Rule 3 fires because the NOT abnormal_fly(tweety) condition succeeds.
KB1 does not derive fly(tweety) because tweety is a penguin.

explanations.text: Since abnormal_fly(tweety) has no supporting rule or fact, NOT abnormal_fly(tweety) succeeds, so rule 3 fires.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
explanations.notes_quote: since abnormal_fly(tweety) cannot be proved, rule 3 fires and: KB1 |~ fly(tweety)

explanations.text: This is a logic program, not a classical KB. The derivation is via negation-as-failure, not classical entailment.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy

explanations.text: NOT abnormal_fly(tweety) succeeds because there is no proof of abnormal_fly(tweety) in KB1.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
explanations.notes_quote: since abnormal_fly(tweety) cannot be proved, rule 3 fires

explanations.text: Being a penguin does not block fly in KB1 — there is no rule making penguins abnormal_fly in KB1.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy

questions.source_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
questions.text: When rule 4 (abnormal_fly(X):-penguin(X),NOT DC_comic(X)) is added to form KB2, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: KB2 |~ fly(tweety) is no longer derivable.
abnormal_fly(tweety) becomes derivable in KB2.
Rule 3's NOT abnormal_fly(tweety) condition now fails.
Adding rule 4 illustrates that logic programming is monotonic.

explanations.text: Once abnormal_fly(tweety) is derivable, the NOT condition in rule 3 fails and fly(tweety) can no longer be derived.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
explanations.notes_quote: KB2 does not derive fly(tweety)

explanations.text: Rule 4 fires because penguin(tweety) holds and DC_comic(tweety) cannot be proved.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
explanations.notes_quote: DC_comic(tweety) cannot be proved, so rule 4 fires and we derive abnormal_fly(tweety).

explanations.text: Since abnormal_fly(tweety) is now derivable, NOT abnormal_fly(tweety) fails, blocking rule 3.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
explanations.notes_quote: Rule 3 can no longer fire (its NOT abnormal_fly condition fails)

explanations.text: This is precisely an example of non-monotonicity — adding rule 4 removes a previously derivable consequence.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
explanations.notes_quote: This demonstrates non-monotonicity: adding a formula to the KB invalidates a previously derivable consequence.

questions.source_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
questions.text: After adding fact DC_comic(tweety) to form KB3, which of the following hold? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: KB3 |~ fly(tweety) is reinstated.
Rule 4 can no longer fire because DC_comic(tweety) is now provable.
abnormal_fly(tweety) remains derivable in KB3.
KB3 demonstrates that non-monotonicity can restore consequences removed by earlier additions.

explanations.text: With abnormal_fly(tweety) no longer derivable, NOT abnormal_fly(tweety) succeeds again and rule 3 fires.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
explanations.notes_quote: KB3 |~ fly(tweety)

explanations.text: Rule 4 requires NOT DC_comic(X); since DC_comic(tweety) is now proved, the NOT condition fails.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
explanations.notes_quote: Now DC_comic(tweety) is provable, so rule 4 can no longer fire.

explanations.text: abnormal_fly(tweety) is no longer derivable — rule 4 is blocked by DC_comic(tweety) being provable.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
explanations.notes_quote: abnormal_fly(tweety) is no longer derivable.

explanations.text: The trilogy shows NM in all directions: add rule → lose consequence; add fact → regain consequence.
explanations.section_ref: Section 1.3: Worked Example: The Tweety Trilogy
explanations.notes_quote: Non-monotonicity is illustrated three ways: adding rule 4 removes fly(tweety); adding fact 5 reinstates it.

questions.text: In the Cinema vs Restaurant example, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Without a preference rule, both Goal(cinema) and Goal(restaurant) are derivable.
Adding Goal(restaurant) > Goal(cinema) makes Goal(restaurant) the unique non-monotonic consequence.
Without preferences the KB is inconsistent.
Preferences resolve dilemmas where multiple conflicting goals are derivable.

explanations.text: Both rules fire given birthday and desire to celebrate, creating a genuine dilemma.
explanations.notes_quote: Both Goal(cinema) and Goal(restaurant) are derivable — a genuine dilemma.

explanations.text: The preference fact strictly orders the two goals, resolving the dilemma in favour of restaurant.
explanations.notes_quote: resolves the dilemma: Goal(restaurant) becomes the unique non-monotonic consequence.

explanations.text: The KB is not inconsistent — it simply has two competing derivable goals, which is a dilemma, not a contradiction.
Preferences are a key tool in non-monotonic reasoning for selecting among competing conclusions.
explanations.notes_quote: Adding the preference fact resolves the dilemma.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly motivate the maxiconsistent approach? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Classical logic is monotonic: KB |= alpha implies KB union {beta} |= alpha.
An inconsistent classical KB entails every formula (ex falso quodlibet).
The maxiconsistent approach works with maximal consistent subsets to avoid explosion.
Classical logic is already non-monotonic so no special treatment is needed.

explanations.text: Monotonicity of classical logic is one of the key properties the maxiconsistent approach must work around.
explanations.notes_quote: Classical logic is monotonic: KB |= alpha implies KB union {beta} |= alpha.

explanations.text: Ex falso quodlibet is precisely why an inconsistent KB is useless in classical logic.
explanations.notes_quote: when a classical KB is inconsistent, every formula follows (ex falso quodlibet).

explanations.text: By working with maximal consistent subsets, the approach avoids the explosion of the full inconsistent KB.
explanations.notes_quote: The maxiconsistent approach avoids this by working with maximal consistent subsets of the KB.

explanations.text: Classical logic is monotonic, not non-monotonic. Special techniques are needed.

questions.text: Regarding the subclass principle applied to the Tweety example (KB2 with penguin(X)->neg fly(X) added), which are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The subclass principle justifies preferring the penguin rule over the bird rule.
KB2 classically entails both fly(tweety) and neg fly(tweety).
The subclass principle says rules for a subclass take priority over rules for the superclass.
The subclass principle says rules for the superclass always override subclass rules.

explanations.text: Penguins are a subclass of birds, so the penguin-specific rule is preferred.
explanations.notes_quote: The subclass principle justifies preferring rule 4 over rule 3: rules expressing properties of a subclass (penguins) take priority.

explanations.text: Using rules 1,2,3 gives fly(tweety); using rules 1,4 gives neg fly(tweety) — a contradiction.
explanations.notes_quote: KB2 classically entails both fly(tweety) (via 1,2,3) and neg fly(tweety) (via 1,4) — a contradiction.

explanations.text: This is the definition of the subclass principle as stated in the notes.
explanations.notes_quote: rules expressing properties of a subclass (penguins) take priority over rules expressing properties of the superclass (birds).

explanations.text: This is the reverse of the subclass principle — more specific (subclass) rules take priority.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe a stratified knowledge base? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Formulas in the same stratum have the same priority (are equivalent under the ordering).
A formula in stratum S_i has strictly higher priority than one in S_j when i < j.
Temporal ordering (more recent = higher priority) is a valid criterion for stratification.
All formulas must be in the same stratum for the procedure to work.

explanations.text: Formulas in the same equivalence class (stratum) have the same priority.
explanations.notes_quote: alpha, beta in S_i iff alpha approx beta (same equivalence class)

explanations.text: Lower stratum index means higher priority in the stratified ordering.
explanations.notes_quote: alpha in S_i, beta in S_j, i < j implies alpha > beta (higher stratum = higher priority)

explanations.text: Temporal ordering is explicitly listed as a valid criterion for the priority ordering.
explanations.notes_quote: Other criteria for ordering include: temporal ordering (more recently acquired information has higher priority).

explanations.text: The whole point of stratification is to distribute formulas across multiple strata with different priorities.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the procedure for computing preferred mcs? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Start by finding all maximal consistent subsets of S_1 (the highest-priority stratum).
At each step, maximally consistently extend with formulas from the next stratum.
Each preferred mcs E_1 union ... union E_n must be such that each prefix is a maximal consistent subset of the corresponding union of strata.
The procedure starts with the lowest-priority stratum and works upward.

explanations.text: Step 1 of the procedure is to find all maximal consistent subsets of S_1.
explanations.notes_quote: Find all maximal consistent subsets E_1, ..., E_m of S_1.

explanations.text: Each extension is then maximally consistently extended with the next stratum's formulas.
explanations.notes_quote: For each E_i, maximally consistently extend with formulas from S_2.

explanations.text: This is the formal definition of a preferred mcs.
explanations.notes_quote: E_1 union ... union E_i is a maximal (under set inclusion) consistent subset of S_1 union ... union S_i

explanations.text: The procedure works top-down from the highest-priority stratum (S_1), not bottom-up.

questions.text: For S1={a, a->b, neg b}, S2={c, neg c}, S3={d}, which of the following are correct about the preferred mcs? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: There are exactly three maximal consistent subsets of S1.
Each mcs of S1 is extended to two subsets when processing S2={c, neg c}.
There are exactly six preferred mcs in total after processing all strata.
d is included in every preferred mcs because it is consistent with all of them.

explanations.text: {a,a->b}, {a,neg b}, {a->b,neg b} are the three mcs of S1.
explanations.notes_quote: So E_1={a,a->b}, E_2={a,neg b}, E_3={a->b,neg b}.

explanations.text: S2 contains c and neg c which are mutually inconsistent, so each mcs of S1 branches into two extensions.
3 mcs of S1 x 2 extensions each from S2 = 6 total preferred mcs.
explanations.notes_quote: These six sets are the preferred mcs of KB.

explanations.text: d from S3 is consistent with all six partial extensions, so it is added to all.
explanations.notes_quote: extend each with S_3={d} (consistent with all)

questions.source_ref: Section 2.5: Credulous and Sceptical Consequence Relations
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe credulous and sceptical consequence? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: KB |~_cred alpha holds if alpha follows from at least one preferred mcs.
KB |~_scept alpha holds if alpha follows from all preferred mcs.
The bottom (contradiction) can be a credulous consequence if the KB is inconsistent enough.
A sceptical consequence is also a credulous consequence.

explanations.text: Credulous consequence requires existence of at least one preferred mcs that entails alpha.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.5: Credulous and Sceptical Consequence Relations
explanations.notes_quote: KB |~_cred alpha iff there exists E in mcs(KB) such that E |= alpha

explanations.text: Sceptical consequence requires all preferred mcs to entail alpha.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.5: Credulous and Sceptical Consequence Relations
explanations.notes_quote: KB |~_scept alpha iff for all E in mcs(KB): E |= alpha

explanations.text: No individual preferred mcs is inconsistent, so the contradiction is never a credulous consequence.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.5: Credulous and Sceptical Consequence Relations
explanations.notes_quote: No individual preferred mcs is inconsistent, so bot is not a credulous consequence.

explanations.text: If alpha follows from all preferred mcs, it certainly follows from at least one.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.5: Credulous and Sceptical Consequence Relations

questions.source_ref: Section 2.5: Credulous and Sceptical Consequence Relations
questions.text: In the six-mcs example (S1={a,a->b,neg b}, S2={c,neg c}, S3={d}), which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: d is a sceptical consequence.
c is a sceptical consequence.
neg c is a credulous consequence.
bot (contradiction) is a credulous consequence.

explanations.text: d appears in every one of the six preferred mcs, so it is a sceptical consequence.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.5: Credulous and Sceptical Consequence Relations
explanations.notes_quote: d is in every preferred mcs => d is a sceptical consequence.

explanations.text: c appears in only three of the six mcs (those with c from S2), so it is credulous but not sceptical.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.5: Credulous and Sceptical Consequence Relations
explanations.notes_quote: a, neg b, c, neg c are credulous (in at least one) but not sceptical.

explanations.text: neg c appears in at least one preferred mcs (those extended with neg c from S2).
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.5: Credulous and Sceptical Consequence Relations
explanations.notes_quote: a, neg b, c, neg c are credulous (in at least one) but not sceptical.

explanations.text: No preferred mcs is inconsistent, so the contradiction is never a credulous consequence.
explanations.section_ref: Section 2.5: Credulous and Sceptical Consequence Relations
explanations.notes_quote: bot is not credulous.

questions.source_ref: Section 3.1: Structure of a Default Theory
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the structure of a default theory (W, Defaults)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: W is a consistent set of classical well-formed formulas.
A default rule alpha_1,...,alpha_n => beta means: if the antecedents hold, then typically beta.
A default with antecedent top (truth) is always applicable.
W may be inconsistent as long as the defaults compensate.

explanations.text: W must be a consistent set of classical formulas — it is the factual world description.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Structure of a Default Theory
explanations.notes_quote: W — a consistent set of classical well-formed formulas (factual world description).

explanations.text: Default rules express typical/usual conclusions given their antecedents.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Structure of a Default Theory
explanations.notes_quote: if alpha_1 and ... and alpha_n, then usually / typically / under normal circumstances beta.

explanations.text: Top is always true, so a default with antecedent top requires no prerequisite.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Structure of a Default Theory
explanations.notes_quote: A default with antecedent top (truth) is always applicable (requires no prerequisite).

explanations.text: W must be consistent. The entire approach assumes W does not already lead to contradiction.
explanations.section_ref: Section 3.1: Structure of a Default Theory

questions.text: Which conditions must a set E satisfy to be a default extension of (W, Defaults)? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: E = Cn(W union the consequents of all applicable defaults).
E must be consistent (E does not derive bot).
E must be maximal under set inclusion among sets satisfying conditions 1 and 2.
E must include the consequents of all defaults regardless of whether their antecedents hold.

explanations.text: E is the classical closure of W plus the consequents of defaults whose antecedents are in E.
explanations.notes_quote: E = Cn(W union consequents of applicable defaults)

explanations.text: Extensions must be consistent — this prevents trivial explosion.
explanations.notes_quote: E does not derive bot (E is consistent)

explanations.text: Maximality ensures we apply as many defaults as possible without contradiction.
explanations.notes_quote: E is maximal (under set inclusion) satisfying 1 and 2

explanations.text: Only defaults whose antecedents are in E contribute their consequents — applicability is required.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the procedure for constructing default extensions? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Start with Cn(W).
Add the consequent of an applicable default only if it is consistent with the current set.
Different choices of defaults to apply first can yield different extensions.
All defaults must be applied in a fixed predetermined order.

explanations.text: The procedure initialises with the classical closure of the world knowledge.
explanations.notes_quote: Start with Cn(W).

explanations.text: Consistency checking prevents contradictory consequents from being added.
explanations.notes_quote: add consequent(d_i) if consistent.

explanations.text: The order in which defaults are selected can lead to different extensions.
explanations.notes_quote: Different choices of defaults yield different extensions.

explanations.text: Without a priority ordering, defaults can be applied in any order, potentially yielding multiple extensions.

questions.text: In the Tweety default example with D1=bird(X)=>fly(X), D2=penguin(X)=>neg fly(X), D3=top=>wings(X), and no priority ordering, which are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: There are two default extensions: one containing fly(tweety) and one containing neg fly(tweety).
wings(tweety) is a sceptical consequence (in both extensions).
fly(tweety) is a sceptical consequence.
neg fly(tweety) is a credulous consequence.

explanations.text: Applying D1 first gives fly(tweety) in E1; applying D2 first gives neg fly(tweety) in E2.
explanations.notes_quote: E1 = Cn(W union {wings(tweety), fly(tweety)}), E2 = Cn(W union {wings(tweety), neg fly(tweety)})

explanations.text: D3 (top=>wings) is applied in both extensions, so wings(tweety) is in both.
explanations.notes_quote: wings(tweety) is in both extensions => sceptical consequence.

explanations.text: fly(tweety) is only in E1, not E2, so it is credulous but not sceptical.
explanations.notes_quote: fly(tweety) and neg fly(tweety) are each in one extension only => credulous but not sceptical.

explanations.text: neg fly(tweety) is in E2, so it is a credulous consequence.
explanations.notes_quote: fly(tweety) and neg fly(tweety) are each in one extension only => credulous but not sceptical.

questions.text: With priority ordering D2 > D1 (penguin rule beats bird rule) in the Tweety example, which are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: There is a single extension containing neg fly(tweety).
neg fly(tweety) is both a credulous and sceptical consequence.
fly(tweety) is neither a credulous nor a sceptical consequence.
The priority ordering creates two extensions instead of one.

explanations.text: With D2 applied first, neg fly(tweety) blocks D1, leaving only one extension.
explanations.notes_quote: Single extension: E2 = Cn(W union {wings(tweety), neg fly(tweety)})

explanations.text: With only one extension, neg fly(tweety) follows from all extensions (sceptical) and at least one (credulous).
explanations.notes_quote: neg fly(tweety) is both a sceptical and credulous consequence

explanations.text: fly(tweety) is blocked in the single extension, so it is in no extension.
explanations.notes_quote: fly(tweety) is neither.

explanations.text: Priority ordering reduces the number of extensions — here to exactly one, not two.

questions.text: For W={a,a->b,c} and defaults (1:top=>neg c, 2:b=>d, 3:d=>neg a, 4:a=>neg d) with ordering 1>2>3>4, which are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Default 1 is skipped because neg c is inconsistent with c in W.
Default 2 fires because b is in Cn(W).
Default 3 is skipped because neg a would be inconsistent with a already derived.
The single extension is Cn(W union {d}).

explanations.text: c is in W, so neg c would be immediately inconsistent — default 1 is skipped.
explanations.notes_quote: Default 1: antecedent top holds, but consequent neg c is inconsistent with c in W. Skip.

explanations.text: a->b and a are both in W, so b is in Cn(W), making default 2 applicable.
explanations.notes_quote: Default 2: antecedent b: since a, a->b in W, we have b in Cn(W). Add d.

explanations.text: a is in Cn(W union {d}) and neg a would contradict it, so default 3 is skipped.
explanations.notes_quote: Default 3: would add neg a; but a in Cn(W union {d}) — inconsistent. Skip.

explanations.text: After applying only default 2, no other defaults can fire consistently.
explanations.notes_quote: Single extension: Cn(W union {d}).

questions.text: For W={neg a or neg b or neg c, d->neg c} and defaults (1:top=>a, 2:a=>d, 3:top=>c, 4:d=>b) with NO ordering, which are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: There are two default extensions.
E1 = Cn(W union {a,d,b}).
E2 = Cn(W union {a,c}).
With ordering 1>2>3>4, there is still more than one extension.

explanations.text: Without ordering, two orderings of default application lead to two distinct extensions.
explanations.notes_quote: Without ordering — two extensions

explanations.text: Applying 1,2,4 in order gives E1=Cn(W union {a,d,b}).
explanations.notes_quote: E1 = Cn(W union {a,d,b})

explanations.text: Applying 1 then 3 gives E2=Cn(W union {a,c}); default 2 is blocked because d->neg c and c forces neg d.
explanations.notes_quote: E2 = Cn(W union {a,c})

explanations.text: With ordering 1>2>3>4, only one extension results: Cn(W union {a,d,b}).
explanations.notes_quote: With ordering 1>2>3>4 — one extension only: Cn(W union {a,d,b}).

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the core idea of argumentation-based non-monotonic reasoning? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Non-monotonic reasoning can be viewed as a decision procedure for choosing between arguments for conflicting claims.
Arguments are built from the KB using premises and rules, then related by attacks.
The claims of winning arguments correspond to the non-monotonic consequences.
The argumentative approach requires abandoning all three non-monotonic logics.

explanations.text: This is Dung's key insight from 1995.
explanations.notes_quote: Non-monotonic reasoning can be viewed as a decision procedure for choosing between arguments for conflicting claims.

explanations.text: Arguments are constructed from KB elements and then related by an attack relation.
explanations.notes_quote: Arguments are built from the KB (using premises and rules) and related by attacks.

explanations.text: Evaluating winning arguments yields the non-monotonic consequences.
explanations.notes_quote: Evaluating which arguments are winning yields the non-monotonic consequences.

explanations.text: The argumentative approach unifies and characterises the three NM logics — it does not replace them.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe a Dung argumentation framework AF = <AR, attacks>? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: AR is a set of arguments.
attacks is a binary relation on AR x AR.
attacks(A,B) means A is a counter-argument to (attacks) B.
The attack relation must be symmetric.

explanations.text: AR is the set of all arguments in the framework.
explanations.notes_quote: AR is a set of arguments

explanations.text: The attack relation is a subset of AR x AR.
explanations.notes_quote: attacks ⊆ AR × AR is a binary relation.

explanations.text: attacks(A,B) means A attacks (is a counter-argument to) B.
explanations.notes_quote: attacks(A,B) means A is a counter-argument to B (attacks B).

explanations.text: The attack relation is not required to be symmetric — A can attack B without B attacking A.

questions.source_ref: Section 4.3: Constructing Arguments from Logic Programs
questions.text: In the logic programming argumentative framework, which of the following are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Argument A attacks argument B if A's claim X challenges an assumption NOT X in B.
In KB2, argument A2 (claiming abnormal_fly(tweety)) attacks A1 (which assumes NOT abnormal_fly(tweety)).
An unattacked argument is always winning.
An argument attacked by a winning argument is itself winning.

explanations.text: The attack rule for LP is that a claim X defeats an assumption NOT X in another argument.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.3: Constructing Arguments from Logic Programs
explanations.notes_quote: Argument A attacks argument B if A's claim X challenges an assumption in B of the form NOT X.

explanations.text: A2 proves abnormal_fly(tweety), directly challenging A1's NOT abnormal_fly(tweety) assumption.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.3: Constructing Arguments from Logic Programs
explanations.notes_quote: A2 attacks A1: A2's claim abnormal_fly(tweety) challenges A1's assumption NOT abnormal_fly(tweety).

explanations.text: An unattacked argument has no challenger, so it is winning by Rule 1 (all zero attackers are OUT).
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.3: Constructing Arguments from Logic Programs
explanations.notes_quote: A2 is unattacked => A2 is winning.

explanations.text: An argument attacked by a winning argument is losing (OUT), not winning.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.3: Constructing Arguments from Logic Programs
explanations.notes_quote: A1 is attacked by a winning argument => A1 is losing.

questions.source_ref: Section 4.4: Constructing Arguments from Classical KBs
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe arguments from a stratified classical KB? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: An argument is a tuple [Delta, alpha] where Delta is a minimal consistent subset of KB that classically entails alpha.
Argument A=[Delta_1, alpha] attacks B=[Delta_2, beta] if alpha negates a premise in Delta_2.
An attack from A to B succeeds if the attacked premise in B is not ordered above the attacking premises in A.
An attack always succeeds regardless of the priority ordering of premises.

explanations.text: Arguments from classical KBs are minimal entailing subsets with their claim.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.4: Constructing Arguments from Classical KBs
explanations.notes_quote: Delta ⊆ KB, Delta |= alpha; No proper subset of Delta classically entails alpha; Delta is consistent.

explanations.text: The attack rule for classical KBs targets premises directly.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.4: Constructing Arguments from Classical KBs
explanations.notes_quote: A=[Delta_1,alpha] attacks B=[Delta_2,beta] if the claim alpha of A negates a premise in Delta_2.

explanations.text: Higher-priority premises in the attacked argument can cancel the attack.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.4: Constructing Arguments from Classical KBs
explanations.notes_quote: the attack from A to B is cancelled if B uses higher-priority premises than A.

explanations.text: Priority ordering can cancel attacks — higher-priority premises in B defeat lower-priority attacks from A.
explanations.section_ref: Section 4.4: Constructing Arguments from Classical KBs

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe when and how preferences are applied? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Preferences are needed when two arguments attack each other symmetrically (a genuine dilemma).
If argument A is preferred to B, the attack from B to A is cancelled.
If argument A is preferred to B, only the attack from A to B survives.
If A is preferred to B, both attacks A->B and B->A are cancelled.

explanations.text: Preferences resolve symmetric dilemmas where neither argument can defeat the other without external ordering.
explanations.notes_quote: Preferences are needed when two arguments attack each other symmetrically (a genuine dilemma).

explanations.text: The weaker argument's attack on the stronger is cancelled by the preference.
explanations.notes_quote: If argument A is preferred to (stronger than) argument B (i.e., A > B), then the attack from B to A is cancelled.

explanations.text: Only the attack from the preferred argument to the non-preferred one survives.
explanations.notes_quote: Only the attack from A to B survives.

explanations.text: Only the weaker argument's attack is cancelled; the stronger argument's attack remains.

questions.text: Which of the following are valid sources of preferences over arguments? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Goal/decision preferences: Goal(restaurant) > Goal(cinema) makes the restaurant argument preferred.
Priority over defaults: d_i > d_j means arguments built using d_i defeat those built using d_j.
Stratification: premises in higher strata defeat premises in lower strata.
Alphabetical ordering of argument names.

explanations.text: Goal preferences from the decision/preference framework directly order arguments.
explanations.notes_quote: If Goal(restaurant) > Goal(cinema), then the argument for restaurant is preferred over the argument for cinema.

explanations.text: Default priority ordering translates directly into argument preference.
explanations.notes_quote: Priority over defaults: d_i > d_j means arguments built using d_i defeat arguments built using d_j.

explanations.text: Stratification of classical formulas provides the third source of argument preference.
explanations.notes_quote: Stratification of classical formulas: Premises in higher strata defeat premises in lower strata.

explanations.text: Alphabetical ordering of argument labels has no semantic significance in the framework.

questions.text: What are the steps of the general argumentative procedure applicable to all three NM logics? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Construct arguments from the KB using facts and rules/defaults/classical formulas.
Identify attacks between arguments.
Apply preferences to decide which attacks succeed.
The claims of winning arguments become the non-monotonic consequences.

explanations.text: Step 1: Build arguments from the KB.
explanations.notes_quote: Construct arguments from the KB (using facts and rules/defaults/classical formulas).

explanations.text: Step 2: Identify which arguments undermine assumptions or premises of others.
explanations.notes_quote: Identify attacks (an argument attacks another if its claim undermines an assumption or premise).

explanations.text: Step 3: Use preference orderings to filter successful attacks.
explanations.notes_quote: Apply preferences (from orderings over rules/formulas/goals) to decide which attacks succeed.

explanations.text: Step 5: The claims of winning arguments are the NM consequences.
explanations.notes_quote: Claims of winning arguments = non-monotonic consequences of the KB.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the labelling function in an AF? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A labelling assigns each argument one of three labels: IN, OUT, or UNDEC.
IN means the argument is winning (accepted).
OUT means the argument is losing (rejected).
UNDEC arguments are those that have no attackers.

explanations.text: The labelling is a total function from arguments to the three-label set.
explanations.notes_quote: A labelling is a total function L: A -> {IN, OUT, UNDEC}

explanations.text: IN indicates a winning, justified, accepted argument.
explanations.notes_quote: IN = winning (justified, accepted)

explanations.text: OUT indicates a losing, overruled, rejected argument.
explanations.notes_quote: OUT = losing (overruled, rejected)

explanations.text: Arguments with no attackers are IN (by Rule 1 vacuously). UNDEC applies when no attacker is IN but at least one is UNDEC.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly state the three labelling rules for a legal labelling? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: Rule 1: x is labelled IN if all attackers of x are OUT.
Rule 2: x is labelled OUT if at least one attacker of x is IN.
Rule 3: x is labelled UNDEC if no attacker is IN and at least one attacker is UNDEC.
Rule 1: x is labelled IN only if it has no attackers at all.

explanations.text: Rule 1 includes the vacuous case where x has no attackers — all zero attackers are trivially OUT.
explanations.notes_quote: Rule 1: All attackers of x are OUT => x is IN

explanations.text: A single IN attacker is enough to make x OUT.
explanations.notes_quote: Rule 2: At least one attacker of x is IN => x is OUT

explanations.text: UNDEC requires no IN attacker but at least one UNDEC attacker.
explanations.notes_quote: Rule 3: No attacker of x is IN, AND at least one attacker of x is UNDEC => x is UNDEC

explanations.text: Rule 1 applies when all attackers are OUT, which includes the case of no attackers (vacuously true).
explanations.notes_quote: Special case: if x has no attackers, then Rule 1 applies vacuously — x must be IN.

questions.text: In the five-argument framework (A5 attacks A1; A2 unattacked; A3 and A4 attack each other), which labellings are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A5 is labelled IN because it has no attackers.
A1 is labelled OUT because its attacker A5 is IN.
A3 and A4 are both labelled UNDEC.
A2 is labelled OUT because A3 and A4 are fighting each other.

explanations.text: A5 has no attackers, so Rule 1 applies vacuously: A5 is IN.
explanations.notes_quote: A5: none -> IN — Rule 1 (vacuously: all zero attackers are OUT)

explanations.text: A1's only attacker is A5 which is IN, so Rule 2 applies: A1 is OUT.
explanations.notes_quote: A1: A5 (IN) -> OUT — Rule 2: has an IN attacker

explanations.text: A3 and A4 attack each other; neither has an IN attacker, each has an UNDEC attacker, so Rule 3 applies to both.
explanations.notes_quote: A3: A4 (UNDEC) -> UNDEC; A4: A3 (UNDEC) -> UNDEC

explanations.text: A2 is unattacked and therefore IN by Rule 1 (vacuously).
explanations.notes_quote: A2: none -> IN — Rule 1 (vacuously)

questions.text: For a symmetric dilemma x <-> y (x attacks y and y attacks x), which of the following are legal labellings? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: L1: x=UNDEC, y=UNDEC
L2: x=IN, y=OUT
L3: x=OUT, y=IN
L4: x=IN, y=IN

explanations.text: Both UNDEC: each has an UNDEC attacker and no IN attacker, satisfying Rule 3.
explanations.notes_quote: L1: UNDEC, UNDEC — Yes — Rule 3 for both

explanations.text: x=IN (attacker y is OUT satisfies Rule 1); y=OUT (attacker x is IN satisfies Rule 2).
explanations.notes_quote: L2: IN, OUT — Yes

explanations.text: Symmetric to L2 with roles reversed — also a valid legal labelling.
explanations.notes_quote: L3: OUT, IN — Yes — symmetric to L2

explanations.text: If x=IN then y should be OUT (Rule 2: y has IN attacker x). Similarly if y=IN then x should be OUT. Both IN violates Rule 2.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly characterise preferred labellings? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A preferred labelling maximises the set of IN-labelled arguments.
Preferred labellings correspond to credulous semantics.
A framework may have multiple preferred labellings.
The grounded labelling is always a preferred labelling.

explanations.text: A labelling is preferred if no other legal labelling has a strict superset of IN arguments.
explanations.notes_quote: no other legal labelling makes more arguments IN.

explanations.text: Preferred labellings are the credulous labellings — they maximise winning arguments.
explanations.notes_quote: Preferred labellings are the credulous labellings — they maximise the set of winning arguments.

explanations.text: Each possible resolution of a dilemma can yield a different preferred labelling.
explanations.notes_quote: A framework may have multiple preferred labellings (one for each possible resolution of dilemmas).

explanations.text: The grounded labelling is the most sceptical (fewest IN arguments) and is generally not a preferred labelling unless the framework has no dilemmas.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly characterise the grounded labelling? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The grounded labelling is the unique legal labelling with a minimal set of IN arguments.
It corresponds to the sceptical consequence relation.
It corresponds to the least fixed point of F_AF.
There can be multiple grounded labellings in any AF.

explanations.text: The grounded labelling minimises IN arguments — no other legal labelling has fewer IN arguments.
explanations.notes_quote: The grounded labelling L is unique and satisfies: for all other legal labellings L*, the set of IN arguments under L* is not a strict subset of the IN arguments under L.

explanations.text: The grounded labelling gives the sceptical consequences — claims in IN only if they win in the most conservative evaluation.
explanations.notes_quote: The grounded labelling is the sceptical labelling.

explanations.text: The grounded extension (least fixed point of F_AF) corresponds to the grounded labelling.
explanations.notes_quote: It corresponds to the grounded extension (least fixpoint of F_AF) from Dung 1995.

explanations.text: The grounded labelling is unique — there is exactly one grounded labelling per AF.
explanations.notes_quote: The grounded labelling L is unique

questions.text: In the V/W/X/Y framework (V attacks W and X; W attacks V and X; X attacks Y), which are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The grounded labelling labels all four arguments UNDEC.
L2 (V=IN, W=OUT, X=OUT, Y=IN) is a preferred labelling.
L3 (W=IN, V=OUT, X=OUT, Y=IN) is a preferred labelling.
There is only one preferred labelling in this framework.

explanations.text: The grounded labelling has an empty IN set — all UNDEC is the most sceptical outcome.
explanations.notes_quote: L1: V=UNDEC, W=UNDEC, X=UNDEC, Y=UNDEC (all undecided — grounded labelling)

explanations.text: L2 is a preferred labelling — V wins the V/W dilemma, X is blocked, Y reinstated.
explanations.notes_quote: L2: V=IN, W=OUT, X=OUT, Y=IN (preferred).

explanations.text: L3 is the symmetric preferred labelling where W wins instead of V.
explanations.notes_quote: L3: W=IN, V=OUT, X=OUT, Y=IN (preferred).

explanations.text: There are two preferred labellings (L2 and L3), corresponding to the two ways to resolve the V/W dilemma.

questions.text: Which of the following are correct about odd cycles in argumentation frameworks? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In an odd cycle (e.g., V->X->W->V), the only legal labelling assigns all arguments UNDEC.
Odd cycles can produce preferred labellings with IN arguments.
An odd cycle is a cycle where the number of arguments is odd.
The UNDEC-only labelling of an odd cycle is both the grounded and the only preferred labelling.

explanations.text: For odd cycles, assigning any argument IN or OUT leads to a violation of the labelling rules.
explanations.notes_quote: For a cycle V->X->W->V (odd cycle), the only legal labelling makes all arguments UNDEC.

explanations.text: In an odd cycle, no legal labelling can assign any argument IN — all must be UNDEC.
An odd cycle is a directed cycle with an odd number of nodes/arguments.
explanations.notes_quote: a cycle V->X->W->V (odd cycle)

explanations.text: Since there is only one legal labelling (all UNDEC), it is trivially both the grounded and the only preferred labelling.
explanations.notes_quote: the only legal labelling makes all arguments UNDEC.

questions.text: In the non-odd cycle example (Y<->Z symmetric attack, V<->W symmetric attack, V attacks X, W attacks X), which are correct? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The grounded labelling labels all arguments UNDEC.
L2 (Z=IN, Y=OUT, W=OUT, V=IN, X=OUT) is a preferred labelling.
L1 (Y=IN, Z=OUT) is a preferred labelling.
Both preferred labellings have the same number of IN arguments.

explanations.text: The grounded labelling assigns all arguments UNDEC in this framework.
explanations.notes_quote: Grounded labelling: all UNDEC.

explanations.text: L2 has Z and V winning, giving two IN arguments.
explanations.notes_quote: L2 (preferred): Z=IN, Y=OUT, W=OUT, V=IN, X=OUT.

explanations.text: L1 has Y winning but no resolution of V/W, so only one IN argument.
explanations.notes_quote: L1 (preferred): Y=IN, Z=OUT, V=UNDEC, W=UNDEC, X=UNDEC.

explanations.text: L1 has one IN argument (Y) and L2 has two (Z and V) — different numbers, but both are preferred because neither's IN set is a superset of the other's.
explanations.notes_quote: L1 has one IN-argument (Y) and L2 has two (Z, V) — but both are preferred because neither's IN-set is a superset of the other's.

questions.source_ref: Section 7: Connecting Arguments to NM Consequences
questions.text: Which of the following correctly map AF concepts to NM logic concepts? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: IN arguments in preferred labellings correspond to credulous consequences.
IN arguments in the grounded labelling correspond to sceptical consequences.
An OUT argument's claim is not a consequence of the KB.
An UNDEC argument's claim is always a credulous consequence.

explanations.text: Preferred labellings give credulous membership — the claim is accepted in at least one consistent position.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7: Connecting Arguments to NM Consequences
explanations.notes_quote: Preferred labelling (IN arguments) | Credulous consequences

explanations.text: The grounded labelling's IN set corresponds to sceptical consequences — accepted in all positions.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7: Connecting Arguments to NM Consequences
explanations.notes_quote: Grounded labelling (IN arguments) | Sceptical consequences

explanations.text: OUT arguments have their claims defeated — the claim is not a consequence.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7: Connecting Arguments to NM Consequences
explanations.notes_quote: Argument is OUT | Claim is not a consequence

explanations.text: An UNDEC argument's claim represents a dilemma — it is neither IN nor OUT, and is not a credulous consequence.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7: Connecting Arguments to NM Consequences
explanations.notes_quote: Argument is UNDEC | Claim is neither in nor out (dilemma)

questions.source_ref: Section 7: Connecting Arguments to NM Consequences
questions.text: Which of the following are correct about how the three NM logics map to the argumentative framework? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: In logic programming, arguments are chains of LP rules and facts; attacks target NOT X assumptions.
In default logic, arguments correspond to applicable default chains; priorities resolve conflicts.
In the maxiconsistent approach, arguments are [Delta, alpha] tuples; attacks negate a premise; preferences come from stratification.
The argumentative characterisation of maxiconsistent reasoning is fully examinable.

explanations.text: LP arguments use NOT X assumptions as attack targets.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7: Connecting Arguments to NM Consequences
explanations.notes_quote: Logic Programming: arguments are chains of LP rules and facts; attacks on NOT X assumptions.

explanations.text: Default logic arguments are constructed from applicable defaults with priority ordering resolving conflicts.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7: Connecting Arguments to NM Consequences
explanations.notes_quote: Default Logic: arguments correspond to applicable default chains; priorities resolve conflicts.

explanations.text: Maxiconsistent arguments are minimal entailing subsets; stratification provides preferences.
explanations.section_ref: Section 7: Connecting Arguments to NM Consequences
explanations.notes_quote: Maxiconsistent: arguments are [Delta, alpha] tuples; attacks negate a premise; preferences from stratification.

explanations.text: The notes explicitly state that the argumentative characterisation of maxiconsistent reasoning is NOT examinable.
explanations.notes_quote: Note: The argumentative characterisation of maxi-consistent non-monotonic reasoning is not examinable.

questions.source_ref: Section 8.1: Core Definitions — Conflict-free and Admissible
questions.text: Which of the following correctly define conflict-free and admissible sets in Dung's framework? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A set S is conflict-free if no argument in S attacks another argument in S.
An argument A is acceptable w.r.t. S if every attacker of A is itself attacked by S.
A conflict-free set S is admissible if each argument in S is acceptable w.r.t. S.
An admissible set may contain arguments that attack each other.

explanations.text: Conflict-free sets have no internal attacks.
explanations.notes_quote: A set S of arguments is conflict-free if there are no A, B in S such that A attacks B.

explanations.text: Acceptability requires that S defends A against all its attackers.
explanations.notes_quote: An argument A in AR is acceptable w.r.t. a set S iff for each B in AR: if B attacks A then B is attacked by S.

explanations.text: Admissibility combines conflict-freeness with collective defence.
explanations.notes_quote: A conflict-free set S is admissible iff each argument in S is acceptable w.r.t. S.

explanations.text: An admissible set must be conflict-free — no internal attacks are permitted.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe preferred extensions in Dung's framework? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A preferred extension is a maximal (under set inclusion) admissible set.
Every AF possesses at least one preferred extension (Corollary 12).
Every stable extension is a preferred extension.
Every preferred extension is a stable extension.

explanations.text: Preferred extensions are maximal admissible sets — no admissible superset exists.
explanations.notes_quote: A preferred extension is a maximal (under set inclusion) admissible set.

explanations.text: Dung's Corollary 12 guarantees at least one preferred extension always exists.
explanations.notes_quote: Corollary 12: Every AF possesses at least one preferred extension.

explanations.text: Lemma 15 states every stable extension is preferred (but not vice versa).
explanations.notes_quote: Lemma 15: Every stable extension is a preferred extension, but not vice versa.

explanations.text: Not every preferred extension is stable — Lemma 15 explicitly states 'not vice versa'.
explanations.notes_quote: Lemma 15: Every stable extension is a preferred extension, but not vice versa.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe stable extensions? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A conflict-free set S is a stable extension iff S attacks each argument not in S.
S is a stable extension iff S equals the set of arguments not attacked by S.
Every preferred extension is a stable extension.
A stable extension must be conflict-free.

explanations.text: Stable extensions attack everything outside themselves.
explanations.notes_quote: A conflict-free set S is a stable extension iff S attacks each argument not in S.

explanations.text: This is Lemma 14 — an equivalent characterisation of stable extensions.
explanations.notes_quote: Lemma 14: S is a stable extension iff S = {A | A is not attacked by S}.

explanations.text: The converse fails — preferred extensions need not be stable (Lemma 15).
explanations.notes_quote: Lemma 15: Every stable extension is a preferred extension, but not vice versa.

explanations.text: The definition of stable extension explicitly requires conflict-freeness.
explanations.notes_quote: A conflict-free set S is a stable extension iff...

questions.source_ref: Section 8.1: Core Definitions — Characteristic Function and Grounded Extension
questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the characteristic function F_AF and grounded extension? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: F_AF(S) = {A | A is acceptable w.r.t. S}.
F_AF is monotonic with respect to set inclusion (Lemma 19).
The grounded extension GE_AF is the least fixed point of F_AF.
The grounded extension is the largest complete extension.

explanations.text: F_AF maps a set S to the set of all arguments acceptable with respect to S.
explanations.notes_quote: F_AF(S) = {A | A is acceptable w.r.t. S}

explanations.text: Lemma 19 establishes the monotonicity of F_AF, which guarantees a least fixed point exists.
explanations.notes_quote: Lemma 19: F_AF is monotonic (w.r.t. set inclusion).

explanations.text: The grounded extension is defined as the least fixed point of the characteristic function.
explanations.notes_quote: The grounded extension GE_AF is the least fixed point of F_AF.

explanations.text: The grounded extension is the least (smallest) complete extension, not the largest.
explanations.notes_quote: The grounded extension is the least (w.r.t. set inclusion) complete extension.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe complete extensions and their relationship to other extension types? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: A complete extension is an admissible set S where every argument acceptable w.r.t. S is already in S.
Each preferred extension is a complete extension.
The grounded extension is the least complete extension.
Every complete extension is a preferred extension.

explanations.text: Complete extensions are admissible sets that are closed under the acceptability condition.
explanations.notes_quote: An admissible set S is a complete extension iff each argument acceptable w.r.t. S belongs to S.

explanations.text: Theorem 25(1) states each preferred extension is a complete extension.
explanations.notes_quote: Each preferred extension is a complete extension (not vice versa).

explanations.text: Theorem 25(2) establishes the grounded extension as the least complete extension.
explanations.notes_quote: The grounded extension is the least (w.r.t. set inclusion) complete extension.

explanations.text: Not every complete extension is preferred — preferred extensions are maximal complete extensions.
explanations.notes_quote: Each preferred extension is a complete extension (not vice versa).

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe the relationship between labellings and extensions? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: The labelling approach provides an equivalent procedural characterisation of AF semantics to the extension-based approach.
Credulous membership: A is IN in some preferred labelling iff A is in some preferred extension.
Sceptical membership: A is IN in the grounded labelling iff A is in all preferred extensions.
The labelling approach and extension-based approach always give different results.

explanations.text: The labelling approach is equivalent to the extension-based approach — both characterise the same semantics.
explanations.notes_quote: The labelling approach provides an alternative, procedural characterisation of AF semantics that is equivalent to the extension-based approach.

explanations.text: Being IN in a preferred labelling corresponds exactly to membership in a preferred extension.
explanations.notes_quote: Credulous membership: Is A contained in an extension? (A is IN in some preferred labelling)

explanations.text: Being IN in the grounded labelling corresponds to membership in all preferred extensions (sceptical membership).
explanations.notes_quote: Sceptical membership: Is A contained in all extensions? (A is IN in the grounded labelling)

explanations.text: The two approaches are equivalent — they give identical results by design.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describe finitary argumentation frameworks? (Select ALL that apply.)

options.text: An AF is finitary if each argument has only finitely many attackers.
For finitary AFs, F_AF is omega-continuous (Lemma 28).
Omega-continuity of F_AF in finitary AFs means the grounded extension can be computed iteratively.
All AFs are finitary by definition.

explanations.text: A finitary AF restricts each argument to having finitely many attackers.
explanations.notes_quote: An AF is finitary iff for each argument A, there are only finitely many arguments in AR which attack A.

explanations.text: Lemma 28 establishes omega-continuity for finitary AFs.
explanations.notes_quote: Lemma 28: If AF is finitary, then F_AF is omega-continuous.

explanations.text: Omega-continuity allows the least fixed point to be reached by iterative application of F_AF starting from the empty set.
AFs can be non-finitary (arguments with infinitely many attackers), which is why finitary is a special condition.
